Sociology is a strange field. In general, people are *theoretically* for replication, but it is well-known that you cannot get a job that way. The benefits of open access are purely via citation counts, but that is partially discounted by hiring committees. There are mixed opinions about open data because sociologists often have antiscientific ethical commitments.

It seems to me that this hyper-scientific approach to social and political "facts" (past and present) dissolves the value of political concepts, behavior, and evaluation. Finally, scientists tend to avoid clarity about political principles, which exposes them to capture by "big" actors in the socio-economic environment. We need a critical mind, not a neutralist one (in a domain that has no neutrality)

"Sociology" as a whole may be a little idiosyncratic of a discipline for a survey of this sort. I'm not sure how useful these responses (mine and other sociologists) will be will be if the survey results are going to be aggregated with non social science disciplines.

I think there's a distinction between publishing replicating studies versus having data available for replication. In my field we are not rewarded for replication, but it is strongly advised that quantitative scholars should be prepared to make data available if requested for replication. Additionally, publishing code for quantitative work is normative so reviewers and editors can check for errors; publishing data is not.

The social sciences DOES have a very bad replication problem.  Several times I've unsuccessfully tried to replicate colleagues' findings with superior (larger N, better measurement, etc.).  Those studies are rarely published as result of a type-1 error.  To the contrary, they typically involve convoluted (moderated mediation, odd exclusionary criteria resulting in poor external validity, etc.), underpowered analyses, often with ill-suited data (often cross-sectional).  Reviewers should catch these things.  I believe the proliferation of open source journals has created pressure to overlook such methodological shortcomings.  A second source of pressure that is maliciously guiding the research environment is the translation of the neoliberal agenda (call it 'woke' if you like) through funding and editorial decisions, including the mob rule effect that has resulted in the withdraw of defensible empirical studies contradicting that neoliberal orthodoxy.

Scientific knowledge valued by academic journals and universities, and academia more generally, is but one of virtually unlimited arenas or circuits of knowledge production and consumption. Objectivity/Subjectivity, Qualitative/Quantitative, and Disciplinary divides serve no real purpose than to further distinguish "scientifc knoweldge" from other epistemoplogy, and in so doing it forges a sense of legitimacy for "scientists" in the Western world who are encoruaged to pursue their intellectual interests only via the mean of legitimzied and institionalized practices and approaches. The goal should be to breach beyond these socially constructed boundaries and pursue uplifting and amplifying approaches to knowledge production and consumption that have been devalued, deprioritized, and neglected in conventional understandings of "scientific" projects across academia.

People fail to make the distinction between values internal to science and values external to science.  Values internal to science, such as decisions about what theory to use, what method to use, how we measure our variables, etc. are relatively value free in the sense that these should be made known to the audience and can be debated if they are the best or not.  Values external to science are the moralistic values that we adopt such as promoting a given political cause.  These are deeply affected by our identities and position within the social structure and cannot be separated from those.  These should be kept out of the scientific discussion as they are unscientific.  But, just because I hold progressive values and want to promote equality, that does not mean I cannot do science objectively if I am transparent about what values I have used internal to science and do not publicize my values external to science.

Whether or not cultural anthropology is a science is controversial. It is generally accepted within the field that ethnographic studies are functionally un-replicable on account of the fact that the researcher is also the basic data-gathering instrument, adding irreducible personality factors to the research. Moreover the cultural situations being researched change over historical time, including in response to the actions of previous ethnographers. Occasionally, anthropologists have gone to the same places and returned with different results; these are generally understood as producing more information about the same 'cultural situation' rather than refuting prior work, or evidence that the 'cultural situation' has changed over time. Most anthropologists would not regard these 'returns' as replication studies. Although cultural anthropologists do have theories that they take with them when they go out to do field research, these rarely take the form of testable hypotheses about which the research will return a true/false evaluation. Therefore preregistration is generally regarded as irrelevant to anthropologists. Data sharing among cultural anthropologists is complicated and controversial; since anthropologists are their own research instrument, data (fieldnotes) tend to take memoiristic forms that would do little to aid other researchers in the same field while creating ethical issues re. the identification of research subjects. This is especially the case where research subjects are involved in politically, legally, or ethically sensitive activities, which is not uncommon. However, there have been modest movements for anthropologists to share more of their fieldnotes.
I don't think scientific community is dominated by "White Men", but that doesn't mean it is not dominated.

I think preregistration is always important for experimental work and is often important for quasi-experimental work. My work is generally not very causal and does not emphasize hypothesis testing, so I have not bothered to preregister it. It's an even more awkward fit for qualitative data and for computational text analysis, which are almost always inductive rather than deductive.

I came to open science late in my career. I'm enthusiastic--I edited a special issue on the topic of registered reports and replications in the leading journal in my field--but I find that, in general, it's younger/earlier career folks who support this movement more than people in my generation.
I do not have confidence in knowing broad trends in my field as a whole. My estimates are more reflective of my advisors and other graduate students in my department.

If you run this survey again, I would recommend adjusting several of these questions -- as someone who is in the process of conducting experimental research, I don't have any publications yet and it was sometimes unclear whether I should answer that I had done certain practices or not. Also, it's a lot of cognitive effort to ask respondents to do the math of adding to 100 and might introduce bias into your measures.

Some nuances that affected my answers but not be quantified: (1) Data sharing regarding qualitative data in my discipline (political science) is extremely complicated, as simple anonymity is often not sufficient for ethical sharing. The ethically correct amount of qualitative data to share can validly be "at the researcher's discretion and at the reviewer's tolerance." (2) While I agree that pre-registration is desirable for experimental work -- both for science as science and science as a profession, I would argue that it is difficult to articulate a case for expanding pre-registration to other realms in my discipline. By design, graduate students in social sciences are asked to shoulder the vast majority of the administrative burden of their research independently. Today, this already constitutes an unacceptable and extremely harmful waste of time. Any changes proposed in the name of "transparency" and "replicability" should be weighed against the uneven effects, across both resource levels and generational cohorts. (3) Open-access, in its current structural form, is a welfare program for rentier corporations with some of the highest profit margins in the world. Unwritten directly and indirectly with a combination of public funds and attrition on researcher time and resources.
The current system of publishing that relies on so much free labor needs to change. However, open access seems to have a reputation for “pay to play.”

Thank you for conducting this survey, I do think that science in general, especially here in the US right now needs to be focused on how to support non-white, non-male faculty members and graduate students. The attitudinal questions about being for or against data sharing, pre-registration, and replication assume that there is a single set of norms that should govern all types of research. One can be highly supportive of these practices for quantitative work, and agnostic or opposed to them in qualitative work at the same time. I expect that asking without specifying the type of research will skew your answers neutral, as people probably have different answers for different disciplines and methods.

This survey has really weird question design, and the lack of opportunity for nuance in answering several of the questions almost made me stop filling it out multiple times. The “hard-core positivism or not” phrasing of many of the questions suggests that this study may under-estimate the degree to which scientists (esp social scientists like me) are committed to truth/realism, but are suspicious of the pure hypothetico-deductive model. I suspect you’ll have trouble sorting out the pragmatists, critical realists, and others who value humility about scientific knowledge from the hard core postmodernists.

Regarding open access to publications, I am under the impression that the social science community seeks to publish in peer reviewed journals, but does not care if the journals are read outside the social science community.  Rather than publishing to better humanity, such as informing policymakers, the goal is for consumption by the academic community to further one's own career.

I do quantitative as well, but in my field, I have been frustrated by the need to adapt my results to a theory. For example, a recent quantitative study (a survey) has not yet been published because it does not employ one of the most common theories; I feel that reviewers have never seen it and find it "weird." I archived it and looked for Feyerabend's "Against Method" :) But anyway, so far, I understand that there is a standard, rigid approach to writing papers that cannot be changed.

I am an archeologist which is often not considered a scientist, but I consider myself a scientist. Due to the nature of archeology I understand I can never know the full “truth” or all of the facts. For us this is because the archeological record is imperfect and incomplete, so we are always working from only a small part of a dataset. I am also an anthropologist first, and so acknowledge that truth is hard to define and often contextual, dependent on cultural norms and historical circumstance. I am in full support of data sharing in my field, but there are also unique barriers. For example, keeping Indigenous North American archeological sites safe often requires keeping the exact locations a secret and not publishing data sets which reference their coordinates. I think open science is incredibly important but we also have to assume that some people who access that information may not have the best intentions. It will be important to protect vulnerable or marginalized communities in the ways we share data and scientific findings and make sure the communities the research affects have a say in how and where the findings are made known.
Thanks for doing a very clear and precise survey!

The questions on open-source were hard to answer.  In Economics, the vast majority of researchers post working papers before publication (at Arxiv, NBER, etc) but publish in non-open source journals.  Yet most researchers then keep their working papers in circulation and/or post PDFs of their papers often in open contravention to the closed-source journals (without much consequence).    In other words, most researchers favor and promote open-access via working papers but the more prestigious journals are still mostly not-open source.

Regarding the questions about preregistration, the only similar procedure in Sociology of which I am aware, is going through Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval.  While I agree with IRB requirement in principle, my experience was that the members of the IRB often did not have a sufficient understanding of "scientific research" to make rational decisions.

Behavioral econ

Once again speaking as an anthropologist, I was not sure how to describe the proportions of researcher attitudes precisely because there are such divergent perspectives. I do agree that our personal positionality does influence our research interests and likely influences how we interpret our results. However I do not think this means that there is no worth in pursuing objectivity, because in the attempt to be objective we can still identify and work to mitigate potential biases. That being said, I am more skeptical of those who have a financial interest (e.g., pharmaceutical research funded in whole or part by a particular drug manufacturer). In anthropology we curate and maintain community relationships which in my opinion *should* influence our work to be more in line with what interests or concerns the community in question has, rather than the researcher imposing their own "objective" viewpoint on the work. So I'm not sure how to personally square that, in that I am comfortable with self-reporting biases or relationships towards community members, which would not set off alarm bells as I read the research, versus financial backing, which in my opinion demonstrably reduces the reliability of research results.

Concerns around open access is largely around cost. If open access were free, I doubt anyone would object.

Though I enjoy curiosity-driven research projects and using both qualitative and quantitative approaches to develop and test social science models, I have become somewhat jaded about publishing. The ease of AI summaries means fewer students (and researchers) read and engage with academic articles. Further, when I review, I've noticed increased careless use of AI in generating text within submitted articles. I have less motivation to spend 200+ hours on a manuscript to prepare it for a top journal. I took a teaching position that allows me to creative research freedom while teaching and mentoring students. I can research what and when I want with little pressure from my department. I had a conversation with another faculty over whether we should conduct a larger series of studies (including replication) and write a book rather than publish in articles. We concluded few would likely read that either. I research more for personal enjoyment/learning now than to publish.

Some of the open access answers are more to do with open access journals being rated less prestigious than other journals in my field. If the prestigious journals were open access I think that would be a good think, but they are currently extremely expensive to make open access if the option is even given.

Scholars studying particular topics are targeted by some scientists who seek to find their data and publish studies to discredit their work. In these cases, generally, there is nothing wrong with the people's work, yet they are targeted because they are studying issues related to women, minorities, and media violence, to name a few. These groups of scholars are disproportionately targeted.
I intentionally skipped all the percentage questions, as I truly have no idea of the proportions for each category. I rarely had those conversations with people in my field.

Science is a process, and i believe in it, and I also believe that it is not ALL that we have to know about our world. The systems that control science - like our government, academic institutions, and societal norms, DO play a role in what gets studied and what is published, etc. If you want to keep doing work, sometimes you have to "obey" enough and "disobey" enough of these systems' unjust rules in order to do as much good science - that helps society - as you possible can.
